1: Date: eleventh year, first day of the month (month unstated). The missing month may indicate a scribal gap or may mean the oracle arrived at a moment when the specific month was less important than the year. Eleventh year of Jehoiachin's exile = 587/586 BCE — the year Jerusalem fell to Nebuchadnezzar. The proximity to Jerusalem's fall is the oracle's immediate context: Tyre's response to news of Jerusalem's destruction is the trigger.
2: 'Tyre has said against Jerusalem, Aha! The gate of the peoples is broken; it has swung open to me; I will be replenished, now that she is laid waste' — Jerusalem as the land-trade gateway. The ancient Via Maris and King's Highway, the two major trade arteries linking Egypt with Mesopotamia and Arabia, both passed through or near Judah. Jerusalem's customs and market monopoly on land-trade was a commercial rival to Tyre's maritime dominance. Tyre's commercial calculation: with Jerusalem's destruction, the land-route trade that formerly enriched Jerusalem would now flow through Tyre's markets. The oracle indicts this predatory commercial opportunism over a fallen covenant people.
3: 'I am against you, Tyre, and I will bring many nations against you as the sea brings up its waves' — the sea metaphor is particularly apt for Tyre, whose maritime power made it the master of the sea. YHWH uses Tyre's own element — the sea and its waves — as the image of the judgment. The 'many nations' (vs. the single instrument of Nebuchadnezzar in Ezekiel 26:7) may reflect the reality that multiple Babylonian campaigns plus Alexander's eventual conquest constituted the fulfillment.
4: 'They will destroy the walls of Tyre and tear down her towers; I will also scrape the soil from her and make her a bare rock' — the name 'Tyre' (Hebrew ṣôr, Phoenician ṣûr) means 'rock.' The city was partly on the mainland and partly on a small island 800 meters offshore. The prediction of a 'bare rock' (sela) literally describes what the island city would become: Alexander the Great (332 BCE), frustrated by his inability to assault the island by sea, demolished the ruins of the mainland city and built a causeway from the debris to the island. This required stripping the mainland ruins down to bedrock — fulfilling the 'bare rock' oracle in a historically precise way 250 years later.
5: 'She will become a place in the middle of the sea for the spreading of nets' — the drying of fishing nets on Tyre's bare rock is attested by ancient geographers after Alexander's conquest. The 'for I have spoken' (ki ani dibbarthu) grounds the prediction in YHWH's word: the authority is the divine speech-act, not historical probability. Tyre was one of the most heavily fortified city-states of the ancient world; its fall was historically improbable at the time of Ezekiel's oracle.
6: 'Her daughters on the mainland will be slain by the sword' — the 'daughters' are the satellite towns on the mainland coast (Ushu, the mainland settlement of Tyre, plus dependent villages). Nebuchadnezzar's 13-year siege (585-572 BCE) successfully destroyed the mainland settlements even if the island city was never fully taken. The mainland daughters/villages were the most vulnerable; the island fortress held out.
7: 'I am bringing against Tyre from the north Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, king of kings, with horses, chariots, cavalry, and a great army' — Nebuchadnezzar is explicitly named and given the title 'king of kings' (melek melachim), the standard Neo-Babylonian royal title reflecting Nebuchadnezzar's position as the paramount monarch of the ancient Near East (cf. Daniel 2:37 where Daniel addresses him with the same title). His approach 'from the north' follows the Fertile Crescent route that all Mesopotamian armies took to reach the Levantine coast: north through Syria then south along the coast.
8: The standard siege-warfare vocabulary of the 6th century BCE: siege works (dayeq), earthen ramp (solelah), large shields (tsinah), battering rams. The siege methods Nebuchadnezzar would employ against mainland Tyre were standard Assyrian and Babylonian siege engineering. The specificity of the siege vocabulary shows Ezekiel's familiarity with contemporary military practice.
9: Battering rams directed against the walls, axes against the towers — the archaeological evidence from Babylonian campaigns (including Lachish reliefs from Sennacherib's earlier campaigns) shows exactly this combination: rams to breach walls at base level, axes/picks to demolish towers. The imagery is technically precise for ancient siege warfare.
10: 'Because of his many horses their dust will cover you; your walls will shake at the noise of horsemen, wheels, and chariots' — the dust of a large cavalry force as an environmental feature of the approach. Neo-Babylonian armies were predominantly infantry but used significant cavalry and chariotry. The 'many horses' signals the scale of the force. The 'walls shaking at the noise' is both literal (vibration from marching armies) and a standard ancient Near Eastern expression for military intimidation.
11: 'His horses' hooves will trample all your streets; he will slaughter your people with the sword and your strong pillars will fall to the ground' — the 'strong pillars' (mattzevot) may refer to the standing stones/pillars at Tyre's famous temple of Melqart (Tyrian Heracles). Herodotus (2.44) describes visiting the great temple of Heracles at Tyre with two columns, one of pure gold and one of emerald. The specific destruction of cultic pillars was standard Babylonian practice for subordinating foreign religious establishments.
12: The plural subject ('they') may shift to the later conqueror Alexander or to the general 'many nations' of Ezekiel 26:3. 'They will plunder your wealth and loot your merchandise; they will break down your walls and destroy your fine houses and hurl your stones, your timber, and your soil into the sea' — this verse is the key to the Alexander fulfillment: the debris of the mainland city (stones, timber, soil) literally thrown into the sea to build the causeway. The verse describes both the destruction and the disposal of the rubble.
13: 'I will put an end to the noise of your songs, and the sound of your harps will be heard no more' — Tyre's famous musical culture: the city's merchants and traders were accompanied by music (Isaiah 23:16: 'take a harp, go about the city, O forgotten prostitute; make sweet melody, sing many songs'). The cessation of music is the standard ancient Near Eastern sign for the end of a city's life — compare the mourning songs for fallen cities in Sumerian and Akkadian literature (the Lamentation over the Destruction of Ur).
14: 'I will make you a bare rock; you will be a place for the spreading of nets and will never be rebuilt, for I the LORD have spoken' — the combination of the bare-rock image and the never-rebuilt prediction. The modern city of Sur/Tyre exists on the ancient island site, technically contradicting the literal 'never rebuilt' prediction. This has generated extensive discussion; interpreters note that the modern city occupies Alexander's causeway connection and the harbor areas, not the ancient island city itself, and that ancient Tyre as a cultural and commercial power was never restored to its former greatness.
15: The cosmic-scale reaction to Tyre's fall: 'Will not the coastlands tremble at the sound of your fall — at the cry of the wounded, at the groaning of the slain, when the sword is wielded in your midst?' The 'coastlands' (iyim) are the Mediterranean maritime cities and islands — the network of ports and trading cities for which Tyre was the hub and center. Tyre's fall disrupted the entire Mediterranean commercial network, producing genuine economic and political shock across the region.
16: 'Then all the princes of the sea will descend from their thrones, remove their robes, and strip off their embroidered garments; they will clothe themselves with trembling' — the mourning protocol of ancient Near Eastern rulers: stripping royal regalia (robes, headdresses, embroidery) as a sign of grief and solidarity with the fallen. The 'princes of the sea' are the rulers of Phoenician, Greek, and other maritime city-states who had built their commercial systems around Tyre's network. Their mourning is both genuine grief and self-interested terror: if Tyre fell, they were vulnerable.
17: The lament (qinah): 'How you are destroyed, you who were inhabited by seafaring men, the renowned city, who was mighty on the sea!' — the qinah meter for a city is unusual; the closest parallel is the lamentations over fallen cities in Mesopotamian literature. The description 'renowned city' translates ir hallulah, possibly 'city of renown/praise' — Tyre's reputation throughout the ancient world was based on its commercial and nautical supremacy. The maritime peoples' existential dependence on Tyre is their grief.
18: 'Now the coastlands tremble on the day of your fall; the islands in the sea are dismayed at your end' — the existential trembling of the maritime world at Tyre's destruction. The commercial network of the Mediterranean and Near Eastern seas was organized around Tyrian weights, measures, trade agreements, and market institutions. Their dismay is rational: the fall of the organizing center of international commerce was an economic catastrophe for all dependent parties.
19: 'When I make you a desolate city like cities that are not inhabited, when I bring up the deep over you and the great waters cover you' — the cosmic waters covering Tyre: the island city returning to the sea from which it rose. The imagery combines the literal (the island in the sea) with the cosmic (the primordial waters of chaos, tehom). A prosperous island city disappearing beneath the sea is both a physical possibility (ancient harbors have subsided; parts of ancient Tyre's harbors are now underwater) and a cosmic metaphor.
20: 'I will bring you down with those who descend to the Pit, to the ancient peoples, and I will place you in the depths of the earth, in places desolate from of old' — the Sheol imagery: Tyre joining the ancient peoples/nations (am olam) who have already descended to the Pit. The 'ancient peoples' in the realm of the dead parallels the Sheol roll-call of Ezekiel 32 (Egypt, Assyria, Elam in the underworld). Tyre's commercial greatness does not exempt it from the common fate of fallen empires. 'So that you will not be inhabited or shine forth in the land of the living' — the absence from the land of the living is the definition of Sheol.
21: 'I will make you an object of terror, and you will be no more. Though you are sought, you will never be found again, declares the Lord GOD' — the final disappearance: Tyre as an object of terror (billahot, H1091, terrors/sudden terrors) but ultimately absent. The 'never found again' is the strongest form of the judgment: the city that was the center of the known world's commercial network becomes unfindable. The commercial and cultural legacy of ancient Tyre effectively disappears after Alexander's conquest; the Phoenician culture and its seafaring traditions do not survive the Hellenistic period as a distinct civilization.
